title: Binary search tree operations

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Binary search tree operations; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Binary search tree operations
sources.claim: A binary search tree maintains an ordering invariant between each node and its subtrees, enabling search proportional to height.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A binary search tree maintains an ordering invariant between each node and its subtrees, enabling search proportional to height.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Deleting a two-child node replaces its key with a valid successor then removes that successor from its original place. Your goal is to implement and test BST operations including zero-, one- and two-child deletion. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Recursive problem design; Linked lists; Depth and breadth traversals; Linear and binary search. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: A binary search tree maintains an ordering invariant between each node and its subtrees, enabling search proportional to height. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, tree nodes and heap buffers need explicit lifetime and bounds; C++ can combine RAII nodes with vector heaps; Java uses object references and PriorityQueue contracts; Python uses objects plus heapq's min-heap functions, while the conceptual invariant stays unchanged.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Duplicate policy must be declared, unbalanced height can become n, and deletion has zero, one, or two-child cases. The draft therefore rejects: Every binary tree supports logarithmic search by comparing keys.

includes: Maintain ordering through search, insertion and deletion and relate shape to cost.
Implement and test BST operations including zero-, one- and two-child deletion.

In C, tree nodes and heap buffers need explicit lifetime and bounds; C++ can combine RAII nodes with vector heaps; Java uses object references and PriorityQueue contracts; Python uses objects plus heapq's min-heap functions, while the conceptual invariant stays unchanged.

outcomes: Implement and test BST operations including zero-, one- and two-child deletion.
